nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving and standing waters in small rivers, brooks, and swamps, generally close to overhanging or emergent vegetation in savannah and humid coastal regions
nativeRange.biotope: West and Central African vegetated savannah waterway

decorPreferences: densely planted aquarium with open swimming lanes at the surface
floating plants (water lettuce, frogbit, salvinia) to diffuse light and provide cover
fine-leaved plants and java moss for spawning sites
dark substrate to enhance the iridescent blue iris
gentle filtration with minimal surface turbulence
tight-fitting lid — jumps readily through any gap

diet.notes: Norman's Lampeye is a micro-carnivore that generally accepts a wide variety of small foods, including high-quality micro-pellets, crushed flake, freeze-dried foods, frozen daphnia, baby brine shrimp, micro worms, and bloodworms. Its very small mouth limits acceptable particle size to roughly 1–2 mm, so foods should be sized or crushed accordingly. A varied diet that includes live or frozen invertebrates tends to bring out the best colour and overall condition.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (ember, neon, green neon)
freshwater dwarf shrimp (Neocaridina) — adult shrimp generally safe; shrimp fry will be eaten
avoidWith: boisterous or fast-moving fish that outcompete at the surface
larger or predatory fish capable of eating small killifish
any tankmate that strips floating plant cover the lampeyes rely on

summary: Norman's Lampeye Killifish is a small, peaceful shoaling fish from vegetated waterways across West and Central Africa, kept for the striking iridescent blue of its eyes. Unlike annual killifish, it lives three to four years without a dormant egg stage and is well suited to a community tank with other small, calm species.
careNotes:
Norman's Lampeye Killifish (Poropanchax normani) is among the more commonly traded members of the African lampeye family Procatopodidae — a group of small surface-dwelling killifish named for the bright, reflective blue iris that catches the light. The older name Aplocheilichthys normani is still found in some trade catalogues, though Poropanchax normani is the currently accepted scientific name. The label 'lampeye killifish' in the trade can sometimes cover related species from the genus Procatopus, so it is worth checking the scientific name when sourcing fish. Unlike the better-known annual killifish of the genus Nothobranchius, Norman's Lampeye is a non-annual species with a lifespan of around 3–4 years; its eggs do not require a dry dormant period to develop.

A tight-fitting lid is essential — like nearly all killifish, lampeyes are accomplished jumpers and will escape through small gaps or open feeding hatches. Groups of at least 6 are strongly recommended, with 8–12 being preferable; isolated individuals tend to be timid, feed poorly, and rarely show full colour. The species is broadly tolerant of community water parameters in the moderately soft to medium-hard, slightly acidic to neutral range, though extremes of hardness or pH should be avoided.

A heavily planted aquarium with floating cover is strongly recommended both for security and to bring out the best colour under diffused, softened light. Surface agitation should be kept gentle; a small sponge filter or low-flow internal filter is a good choice. Because of its small mouth, all foods must be appropriately sized — micro-pellets, crushed flake, and small live or frozen invertebrates all work well. A 20 US gallon (approximately 75 litres) tank with a roughly 60 cm footprint is a sensible minimum for a single-species shoal; community setups benefit from additional length to give the surface-cruising lampeyes room to move above the other occupants.
breedingNotes: Norman's Lampeye is an egg-scatterer that deposits small adhesive eggs — roughly 1.5 mm — among fine-leaved plants and floating roots. The eggs do not require a dry dormant period (diapause) and generally hatch within about 10–14 days at typical aquarium temperatures. For controlled breeding, a small dedicated tank of around 15–25 litres fitted with a java moss mat or a spawning mop tends to work well. Adults will eat both eggs and newly hatched fry, so it is advisable to either remove the parents after spawning or transfer the spawning material to a separate hatching container. The species is reported to spawn readily in community aquariums, but fry rarely survive in that setting without targeted rearing. Fry are very small at hatching and benefit from infusoria or paramecia for the first few days, progressing to baby brine shrimp nauplii and micro worms as they grow. Breeding is generally considered easy to accomplish; the main challenge lies in rearing the tiny fry rather than in triggering spawning. In mature fish, the sexes are distinguishable — males are slimmer, more intensely coloured, and carry somewhat extended dorsal and anal fins, while females are rounder-bodied and more plainly marked.

primary.name: Aquatic Arts — Norman's Lampeye Killifish
primary.notes: Primary trade-source page. Provided adult size (1.2–1.6 in / 3–4 cm), temperature (73–78°F / 23–26°C), pH (6.5–7.0), water hardness (4–15 dKH), minimum tank size (20 US gal for 6+), peaceful temperament, shoaling requirement (6+), carnivore diet, jumping/escape warning, and compatibility notes (safe with adult dwarf shrimp; eats shrimp fry).

additional.name: FishBase — Poropanchax normani
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Procatopodidae, Cyprinodontiformes), maximum size (4.5 cm TL males / 4.0 cm TL females), expanded native-range country list (Senegal through Sudan across West and Central Africa), habitat (small rivers, brooks, swamps with vegetation in savannah), and water parameters (22–26°C, pH 6.5–7.2). Designated secondary SeriouslyFish returned 404 for this species so FishBase was used as primary cross-source.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Poropanchax normani
additional.notes: Cross-checked synonymy (formerly Aplocheilichthys normani Ahl, 1928), lifespan (~3–4 years), shoaling behaviour, egg morphology (≈1.49 mm adhesive demersal eggs, externally fertilised), and conservation status (Least Concern). Used as second secondary because SeriouslyFish (primary designated secondary) 404'd and Practical Fishkeeping 403'd.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer that spawns readily among java moss and fine-leaved plants. Adhesive demersal eggs are approximately 1.5 mm and hatch in around 10–14 days; no dry diapause is required, as this is a non-annual species. Fry are very small and need infusoria or paramecia before graduating to baby brine shrimp nauplii. Triggering spawning is generally straightforward; the main challenge is rearing the tiny fry to a survivable size, particularly in community tanks.
